section.title: Elihu's First Speech
section.sectionRationale: A new voice enters once the three friends fall silent. Elihu, younger and until now quiet out of respect, is angry at Job for justifying himself over God, and angry at the three friends for condemning Job without ever actually refuting him. His opening speech introduces himself as compelled to speak, then turns to Job directly, offering a genuinely new idea: suffering can be God's chastening voice, and there is hope in 'a mediator, one in a thousand,' who can secure a ransom for the sufferer.
section.thesis: Elihu is a distinct voice in the book, neither simply repeating the three friends' failed doctrine of automatic retribution nor fully answering Job's case, but introducing a third possibility: suffering as God's disciplining voice, and a hope in mediation and ransom that reaches toward something Job himself has already longed for.
section.frameNote: Two chapters, eight movements: the narrator's introduction of Elihu's kindled anger; Elihu's explanation for why he must finally speak; his claim to be full of words needing release; his invitation for Job to respond as an equal; his charge that Job is not right in this matter; his teaching that God speaks through dreams and through chastening pain; his striking picture of a mediator who secures a ransom for the sufferer; and his closing call for Job to listen and learn wisdom.

units.label: Elihu's kindled anger
units.note: for justifying himself rather than God

verses.text: So these three men stopped answering Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes.
This {{charah|kindled}} the anger of Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram. He burned with anger against Job for justifying himself rather than God,
and he burned with anger against Job’s three friends because they had failed to refute Job, and yet had condemned him.
Now Elihu had waited to speak to Job because the others were older than he.
But when he saw that the three men had no further reply, his anger was kindled.

units.label: Why Elihu must speak
units.note: there is a spirit in a man

verses.text: So Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite declared: “I am young in years, while you are old; that is why I was timid and afraid to tell you what I know.
I thought that age should speak, and many years should teach wisdom.
But there is a spirit in a man, the breath of the Almighty, that gives him understanding.
It is not only the old who are wise, or the elderly who understand justice.
Therefore I say, ‘Listen to me; I too will declare what I know.’
Indeed, I waited while you spoke; I listened to your reasoning; as you searched for words,
I paid you full attention. But no one proved Job wrong; not one of you rebutted his arguments.
So do not claim, ‘We have found wisdom; let God, not man, refute him.’
But Job has not directed his words against me, and I will not answer him with your arguments.

units.label: Full of words
units.note: like a new wineskin about to burst

verses.text: Job’s friends are dismayed, with no more to say; words have escaped them.
Must I wait, now that they are silent, now that they stand and no longer reply?
I too will answer; yes, I will declare what I know.
For I am full of words, and my spirit within me compels me.
Behold, my belly is like unvented wine; it is about to burst like a new wineskin.
I must speak and find relief; I must open my lips and respond.
I will be partial to no one, nor will I flatter any man.
For I do not know how to flatter, or my Maker would remove me in an instant.

units.label: Elihu invites Job to respond
units.note: I too was formed from clay

verses.text: “But now, O Job, hear my speech, and listen to all my words.
Behold, I will open my mouth; my address is on the tip of my tongue.
My words are from an upright heart, and my lips speak sincerely what I know.
The {{ruach|Spirit}} of God has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.
Refute me if you can; prepare your case and confront me.
I am just like you before God; I was also formed from clay.
Surely no fear of me should terrify you; nor will my hand be heavy upon you.

units.label: You are not right in this matter
units.note: God speaks in one way and in another

verses.text: Surely you have spoken in my hearing, and I have heard these very words:
‘I am pure, without transgression; I am clean, with no iniquity in me.
Yet God finds occasions against me; He counts me as His enemy.
He puts my feet in the stocks; He watches over all my paths.’
Behold, you are not right in this matter. I will answer you, for God is greater than man.
Why do you complain to Him that He answers nothing a man asks?

units.label: God speaks through dreams and pain
units.note: to preserve his soul from the Pit

verses.text: For God speaks in one way and in another, yet no one notices.
In a dream, in a vision in the night, when deep sleep falls upon men as they slumber on their beds,
He opens their ears and terrifies them with warnings
to turn a man from wrongdoing and keep him from pride,
to preserve his soul from the Pit and his life from perishing by the sword.
A man is also chastened on his bed with pain and constant distress in his bones,
so that he detests his bread, and his soul loathes his favorite food.
His flesh wastes away from sight, and his hidden bones protrude.
He draws near to the Pit, and his life to the messengers of death.
Yet if there is a {{melits|messenger}} on his side, one mediator in a thousand, to tell a man what is right for him,
to be gracious to him and say, ‘Spare him from going down to the Pit; I have found his {{kopher|ransom}},’
then his flesh is refreshed like a child’s; he returns to the days of his youth.
He prays to God and finds favor; he sees God’s face and shouts for joy, and God restores His righteousness to that man.
Then he sings before men with these words: ‘I have sinned and perverted what was right; yet I did not get what I deserved.
He redeemed my soul from going down to the Pit, and I will live to see the light.’
Behold, all these things God does to a man, two or even three times,
to bring back his soul from the Pit, that he may be enlightened with the light of life.

units.label: Listen to me and be silent
units.note: for I would like to vindicate you

verses.text: Pay attention, Job, and listen to me; be silent, and I will speak.
But if you have something to say, answer me; speak up, for I would like to vindicate you.
But if not, then listen to me; be quiet, and I will teach you wisdom.”

charah.gloss: To burn, to be kindled with anger. The word describes Elihu's anger four times in the opening verses, both at Job for justifying himself over God and at the three friends for condemning Job without ever actually answering him.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Elihu claims the Spirit of God has made him and given him life and understanding, the same word used of the breath of life in Genesis 2:7, grounding his authority to speak not in age but in this shared divine gift.

melits.gloss: An interpreter, spokesman, or mediator, one who represents another's case. Elihu's picture of 'one mediator in a thousand' who speaks on the sufferer's behalf is one of the most striking mediatorial images in the Old Testament, anticipating the New Testament's one Mediator between God and man.

kopher.gloss: A ransom, a price paid to cover or redeem a life, from the same root as kaphar, to atone. Elihu pictures the mediator declaring 'I have found his ransom,' language that resonates deeply with the sacrificial and substitutionary themes fulfilled in Christ's atoning death.

basic.context: After the three friends run out of arguments, a younger man named Elihu, who has been listening in respectful silence, finally speaks, angry both at Job for justifying himself rather than God, and at the friends for condemning Job without ever actually proving him wrong. Elihu introduces himself at length, then turns to Job with a genuinely new idea, that suffering can function as God's warning voice, given through dreams or through chastening pain, meant to turn a person from pride, and that there is hope through a mediator who can find a ransom for the sufferer's life.
basic.hermeneutics: Elihu should not simply be read as a fourth version of the friends' failed theology, nor as fully authoritative just because he speaks last before God. The book presents him as a distinct voice: God never rebukes Elihu by name alongside the three friends in 42:7-9, but Elihu's own speeches also contain overconfidence, and his role seems to be preparing Job to hear God rather than delivering the final word himself.
basic.originalLanguages: Elihu's anger, charah, burns against both Job and the friends. He claims the ruach, Spirit, of God has made him and given him life, grounding his right to speak in something deeper than age. And his picture of a melits, mediator, who finds a kopher, ransom, for the sufferer reaches toward hope that goes further than anything the three friends offered.
basic.typology: Elihu's 'mediator, one in a thousand,' who finds a ransom to spare a soul from the Pit, is one of the Old Testament's most vivid anticipations of Christ, our one Mediator, who found not merely a ransom but became the ransom Himself.
basic.christ: Elihu's picture of a mediator who intercedes for the sufferer and secures a ransom price so he is spared from the Pit points directly forward to Jesus Christ, described in 1 Timothy 2:5 as 'the one Mediator between God and men,' who gave Himself as a ransom for all, and in Mark 10:45, who came to give His life as a ransom for many.
basic.god: Elihu insists God is greater than man and speaks in more ways than Job has noticed, through dreams, through pain, and through a mediating voice; this is true as far as it goes, though Elihu, like the friends before him, still assumes he can fully explain God's purposes in Job's specific suffering, a confidence God will gently correct in His own speech.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Timothy 2:5-6 names Christ as the one Mediator who gave Himself as a ransom for all. Mark 10:45 has Jesus describe His own mission in ransom language. Hebrews 12:5-11 develops the theme of suffering as fatherly discipline that Elihu introduces here. Job 42:7-9 notably does not include Elihu among the friends God rebukes.
basic.oneStory: Elihu introduces a genuinely new idea into Job's story: a mediator who can secure a ransom and spare a soul from the Pit. The whole Bible moves toward the fulfillment of exactly this hope in Jesus Christ, the one Mediator between God and humanity, who does not merely announce a ransom has been found but becomes the ransom Himself.

context.literary: A new character is formally introduced with his own genealogy, Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, breaking the pattern of three friends and signaling a shift in the book's argument. His first speech spans two full chapters, moving from an extended self introduction into direct address of Job, ending with an invitation for Job to respond that Job never takes up.
context.historical: Elihu's youth and deference to his elders before speaking reflects the ancient Near Eastern value placed on the wisdom of age, which he must explicitly justify overriding by appeal to the Spirit of God rather than years lived, a notable theological move within the culture's own conventions.
context.canonical: Elihu's speeches occupy a unique place in the book, situated between the failed arguments of the three friends and God's own speech from the whirlwind, and his ransom and mediator language in 33:23-24 stands among the Old Testament's clearest anticipations of substitutionary atonement.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce a distinct theological voice, neither simply repeating the three friends' rigid retribution doctrine nor claiming Job's full innocence explains everything, but proposing that suffering can function as God's disciplining and warning voice, while also offering, almost in passing, one of the book's most striking pictures of mediated hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Elihu's claim that suffering can be God's chastening voice is a real and sometimes true pattern described in Scripture, Hebrews 12:5-11, though it is not offered here as a universal explanation for all suffering, including, as the book will show, Job's own case, which had a different origin altogether, chapters 1-2.

debates.question: Is Elihu a positive, corrective voice, a negative one no better than the three friends, or something in between?

views.label: A negative voice, as arrogant as the friends
views.case: Elihu speaks at great length about himself before addressing Job, claims special inspiration, 32:8, and like the friends assumes he can explain Job's suffering; some read his long windedness and self assurance as evidence he is no real improvement.

views.label: A positive, transitional voice preparing for God's speech
views.case: Unlike the three friends, Elihu is never named among those God rebukes in 42:7-9, he introduces the previously unexplored idea of suffering as discipline rather than only punishment, and his emphasis on God's greatness and inaccessibility to human judgment anticipates the whirlwind speeches closely enough that many read him as God's appointed forerunner.

debates.bearing: The absence of Elihu's name from God's rebuke in 42:7-9 is the strongest textual evidence that his role, while imperfect and overconfident in places, is meant to prepare Job for God's appearance rather than to be dismissed alongside the three friends.

debates.question: Does Elihu's mediator in 33:23-24 refer to an angelic being, a human intercessor, or is it messianic prophecy?

views.label: An angelic intermediary in Elihu's own worldview
views.case: In its immediate context, Elihu likely pictures a heavenly messenger or angel who intercedes for a suffering person, a known ancient Near Eastern and later Jewish concept of intercessory angels.

views.label: A pattern that finds its fullest meaning in Christ
views.case: Whatever Elihu's original intent, the language of a mediator who finds a ransom to spare a soul from death maps so precisely onto later biblical revelation that Christian readers have long seen it as a genuine anticipation, fulfilled beyond what Elihu himself could have grasped, in the one Mediator, 1 Timothy 2:5.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: the text's original meaning likely involves a heavenly intercessor, and its fullest, Spirit intended meaning is realized in Christ, in keeping with how the New Testament often reads Old Testament patterns as pointing beyond their immediate context.

study.originalLanguages: Elihu's charah, kindled anger, drives him to break his respectful silence. He grounds his authority not in age but in the ruach, Spirit, of God that gives him life and understanding. And in his most striking image, he pictures a melits, mediator, who secures a kopher, ransom, so that a dying man's soul is spared from the Pit, language that resonates through the whole Bible's story of atonement.

typology.type: The mediator who finds a ransom to spare a soul from the Pit, Job 33:23-24
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the one Mediator between God and man, who gave Himself as a ransom for all, 1 Timothy 2:5-6, Mark 10:45.

study.christ: Elihu's picture of 'one mediator in a thousand' who intervenes on behalf of a suffering, dying man, declaring 'I have found his ransom,' is one of the Old Testament's clearest anticipations of the gospel. What Elihu describes in general terms, a mediator securing a ransom to spare a life from the Pit, the New Testament announces has happened concretely and finally in Jesus Christ, the one Mediator between God and men, who gave Himself as a ransom for all, 1 Timothy 2:5-6, and whose own words describe His mission in identical terms, to give His life as a ransom for many, Mark 10:45.
study.god: Elihu insists, rightly, that God is greater than man and speaks in more ways than people often notice, through dreams, through the discipline of pain, and through mediating voices; yet like the friends before him, Elihu still assumes a confident grasp of exactly how God's dealings with Job must be explained, a confidence the whirlwind speeches will gently but firmly correct.

crossReferences.note: Names Christ as the one Mediator between God and men who gave Himself as a ransom for all, directly echoing Elihu's language.
Jesus describes His mission as giving His life as a ransom for many, the fulfillment of Elihu's mediator picture.
Develops the theme Elihu introduces, that God disciplines those He loves through hardship, for their good.
God's rebuke names only Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar, notably omitting Elihu from the friends corrected.

study.oneStory: Elihu steps into Job's story as a genuinely new voice, proposing that suffering can be discipline rather than only punishment, and offering, almost in passing, one of Scripture's clearest early pictures of a mediator who ransoms a soul from death. The whole Bible carries this thread forward to its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the one Mediator who did not merely find a ransom but became it.

sermon.title: Job: Rebuked in Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon on Job 32:1-37:24 addresses Elihu's introduction and first speech directly, presenting Elihu's view that Job's suffering, though not punishment, served to purge pride from his life, a reading that engages precisely the mediator and discipline themes of Job 33.

quiz.q: Why is Elihu angry, according to 32:2-3?

options.text: At Job for justifying himself rather than God, and at the three friends for condemning Job without ever refuting his arguments.
options.note: Both angers are stated explicitly and directed at different failures in the preceding debate.

options.text: Only at Job, for no stated reason.
options.note: Elihu's anger is directed at both Job and the three friends, for distinct reasons.

options.text: Only at the three friends, for being too gentle with Job.
options.note: Elihu's complaint against the friends is that they condemned Job without actually refuting him, not that they were too gentle.

options.text: At God, for allowing Job's suffering.
options.note: Elihu's anger is directed at the human speakers in the debate, not at God.

quiz.q: On what basis does Elihu claim the right to speak, despite being younger than the others?

options.text: The Spirit of God gives understanding regardless of age; wisdom is not only for the old.
options.note: Verses 8-9 make this argument explicitly, grounding his authority in the Spirit rather than years lived.

options.text: He claims to be older than he actually is.
options.note: Elihu openly states he is young, 32:6, and does not misrepresent his age.

options.text: He claims special revelation that overrides all Scripture.
options.note: He appeals to the Spirit's common gift of understanding, not a claim to override revealed truth.

options.text: He says wisdom belongs only to Job's specific tribe.
options.note: No such claim appears in the text; his argument is about age and the Spirit's gift, not tribal identity.

quiz.q: What new idea does Elihu introduce about suffering in 33:19-30?

options.text: Suffering can function as God's chastening voice, turning a person from pride, with hope offered through a mediator who secures a ransom.
options.note: This is a genuinely new angle, distinct from the friends' simple 'suffering equals punishment for specific sin.'

options.text: Suffering is always direct punishment for a specific named sin.
options.note: This was the three friends' position; Elihu's view of discipline and mediation is more nuanced.

options.text: Suffering has no purpose whatsoever.
options.note: Elihu argues suffering serves the purpose of turning a person from pride and wrongdoing.

options.text: Suffering proves God does not exist.
options.note: Elihu's entire argument assumes and affirms God's active involvement in human suffering.

quiz.q: How does Elihu's picture of a 'mediator, one in a thousand' who finds a 'ransom,' 33:23-24, connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates Christ, described as the one Mediator between God and men who gave Himself as a ransom for all.
options.note: 1 Timothy 2:5-6 and Mark 10:45 use strikingly similar mediator and ransom language fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: It has no connection to anything in the New Testament.
options.note: It is widely recognized as one of the Old Testament's clearest anticipations of mediatorial atonement.

options.text: It proves salvation is achieved by human effort alone.
options.note: The passage emphasizes a mediator acting on the sufferer's behalf, not the sufferer's own effort.

options.text: It teaches that angels, not Christ, are humanity's only hope.
options.note: While Elihu may originally picture an angelic intercessor, the New Testament reveals the pattern's fullest meaning in the one Mediator, Christ.

quiz.q: What is notable about how God treats Elihu in the book's epilogue, 42:7-9?

options.text: God rebukes Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar by name, but does not include Elihu among those corrected.
options.note: This omission is the strongest textual clue that Elihu occupies a different, more preparatory role than the three friends.

options.text: God specifically praises Elihu above all the other speakers, including Job.
options.note: God does not single out Elihu for praise either; he is simply absent from the list of those rebuked.

options.text: God rebukes Elihu even more harshly than the three friends.
options.note: Elihu is not named or rebuked at all in the epilogue.

options.text: Elihu is included among the three friends who must be forgiven by Job.
options.note: Only Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar are named as needing Job's intercessory prayer, 42:8-9.

questions: Elihu waited respectfully before speaking, then felt compelled once he had something true to say. How do you discern the right time to speak up versus the wisdom of staying quiet a while longer?

Elihu suggests that pain can be God's way of turning someone from pride they cannot otherwise see in themselves. Is there a hard season you can now see, in hindsight, refined something in you that comfort never would have touched?
Elihu's picture of a mediator who finds a ransom for a dying soul points forward to Christ. How does knowing Jesus is your Mediator change how you approach God when you feel far from Him?

Elihu claims special insight from the Spirit while still being capable of overconfidence in his own judgment. How do you hold conviction about what you have learned from God alongside real humility about what you might still be missing?
Job never responds to Elihu directly in the text. Have you ever received counsel that was partly right but that you were not yet ready to fully receive? What helped you eventually weigh it fairly?

translationNotes.note: 'My belly is like unvented wine' pictures Elihu's pent up words as fermenting wine pressing against its skin, a vivid image of internal compulsion to speak that the BSB renders literally from the Hebrew idiom.
'Formed from clay' echoes the creation language of Genesis 2:7, and is Elihu's way of leveling the playing field with Job, insisting he speaks as a fellow creature, not a superior judge.

'Messenger' translates melits, which can mean an interpreter, spokesman, or mediator; older English translations sometimes render it 'interpreter,' but 'mediator' better captures its function of representing the sufferer's case and securing a ransom.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 32:1-33:33. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
